preamble.summary: Psalm 65 is a hymn of praise to the God who answers prayer, forgives transgression, and saturates the earth with abundance. It moves through three zones of divine activity: the temple (where praise waits and vows are fulfilled), the cosmic arena (where God stills the roaring seas and silences the tumult of nations), and the agricultural landscape (where God waters the furrows, softens the soil with rain, and crowns the year with goodness so that even the wilderness pastures drip and the hills wrap themselves in joy). The psalm is an integrated vision of God's work from altar to field.
preamble.remarkable: The progression from temple to creation to harvest makes this psalm a comprehensive theology of divine provision. God does not merely rule from a distance — he works the soil. The image in verse 10 of God 'visiting the earth and saturating it' uses agricultural language for divine attention: God tends the world the way a farmer tends a field. The closing images are extraordinary: wagon tracks drip with abundance, pastures are clothed with flocks, and valleys are covered with grain so thick they 'shout for joy and sing.' The entire landscape becomes a congregation.
preamble.friction: The superscription identifies this as a psalm and song of David. Some scholars date it later based on the reference to the temple ('your courts,' verse 5) and the harvest thanksgiving theme. The Hebrew of verse 2 is debated: 'Praise waits silently for you' (dumiyyah tehillah) is an unusual construction — silence as a form of praise. Verse 4 may refer to election ('blessed is the one you choose and bring near') in a priestly or more general sense. The agricultural imagery of verses 10-14 is so specific that some scholars read this as a response to a particular abundant harvest rather than a general thanksgiving.
preamble.connections: The forgiveness of transgressions (verse 4) connects to the Day of Atonement traditions (Leviticus 16). The stilling of the seas (verse 8) echoes the creation narrative (Genesis 1:9-10) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14-15). The vision of the earth producing abundance through divine watering anticipates Isaiah's vision of the desert blooming (Isaiah 35) and the new creation imagery of Revelation 22. Jesus' parable of the sower (Matthew 13) assumes this same theology of God-given agricultural abundance.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm and Song of David.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. A psalm of David. A song.
translator_notes: The double designation mizmor ('psalm') and shir ('song') may indicate a piece that combines instrumental performance with vocal singing.

verses.text_hebrew: לְךָ֤ דֻֽמִיָּ֬ה תְהִלָּ֓ה אֱלֹ֘הִ֤ים בְּצִיּ֗וֹן וּ֭לְךָ יְשֻׁלַּם־נֶֽדֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: Praise waiteth for thee, O God, in Sion: and unto thee shall the vow be performed.
verses.rendering:
Praise waits silently for you, God, in Zion,
and to you the vow will be fulfilled.
verses.expanded_rendering: The opening phrase dumiyyah tehillah ('silence is praise' or 'praise waits in silence') is one of the most theologically dense expressions in the Psalms. Silence before God is itself an act of worship — not the silence of having nothing to say but the silence of being so overwhelmed by God that words have not yet formed. This silent praise is located in Zion, the place of God's dwelling, and is paired with the fulfillment of vows (yeshullam neder), which requires speech and action. The psalm thus begins with both silence and commitment.
translator_notes: The word dumiyyah ('silence, stillness') also appears in Psalm 62:2. Here it modifies tehillah ('praise') — silence as the form praise takes when it is most reverent. Zion as the location centers this psalm in the worship life of Jerusalem.

verses.text_kjv: O thou that hearest prayer, unto thee shall all flesh come.
verses.rendering:
You who hear prayer —
to you all flesh will come.
translator_notes: God is addressed as shomea tefillah ('the one who hears prayer') — a participial title that defines God's character by his responsiveness. The scope is universal: kol basar ('all flesh') will come to this prayer-hearing God. The psalm envisions a worship that extends beyond Israel to encompass all humanity.

verses.text_hebrew: דִּבְרֵ֣י עֲ֭וֺנֹת גָּ֣בְרוּ מֶ֑נִּי פְּ֝שָׁעֵ֗ינוּ אַתָּ֥ה תְכַפְּרֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Iniquities prevail against me: as for our transgressions, thou shalt purge them.
verses.rendering:
When the weight of wrongdoing overpowers me,
you cover over our rebellions.
translator_notes: The shift from singular 'me' (menni) to plural 'our' (pesha'enu) moves from personal to communal confession. The verb gavru ('they overpower, they are too strong') acknowledges that sin is stronger than the sinner — human effort cannot deal with it. The verb tekhaprem ('you cover them, you atone for them') is the verbal root of kippur (atonement). Only God can handle what has overpowered the psalmist.

key_terms.semantic_range: to cover, to atone, to make expiation, to purge
key_terms.note: The root k-p-r carries the weight of the entire sacrificial atonement system. Here it is God who performs the covering — the initiative and the power to deal with sin belong to him.

verses.text_hebrew: אַשְׁרֵ֤י ׀ תִּֽבְחַ֣ר וּתְקָרֵב֮ יִשְׁכֹּ֢ן חֲצֵ֫רֶ֥יךָ נִ֭שְׂבְּעָה בְּט֣וּב בֵּיתֶ֑ךָ קְ֝דֹ֗שׁ הֵיכָלֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is the man whom thou choosest, and causest to approach unto thee, that he may dwell in thy courts: we shall be satisfied with the goodness of thy house, even of thy holy temple.
verses.rendering:
Blessed is the one you choose and bring near
to dwell in your courts.
We will be satisfied with the goodness of your house,
the holiness of your temple.
translator_notes: The verbs tivchar ('you choose') and utqarev ('you bring near') place the initiative with God — access to divine presence is by invitation, not achievement. The satisfaction (nisbe'ah) that comes from the 'goodness of your house' (tuv betekha) uses the same satisfaction language as Psalm 63:6 — presence as feast.

verses.text_hebrew: נ֤וֹרָא֨וֹת ׀ בְּצֶ֣דֶק תַּ֭עֲנֵנוּ אֱלֹהֵ֣י יִשְׁעֵ֑נוּ מִבְטָ֥ח כׇּל־קַצְוֵי־אֶ֝֗רֶץ וְיָ֣ם רְחֹקִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: By terrible things in righteousness wilt thou answer us, O God of our salvation; who art the confidence of all the ends of the earth, and of them that are afar off upon the sea:
verses.rendering:
With awesome deeds of righteousness you answer us,
God of our deliverance,
you who are the confidence of all the far ends of the earth
and the distant seas.
translator_notes: The word nora'ot ('awesome things, fearsome acts') describes God's answers to prayer as overwhelming in their power. These answers come be-tsedeq ('in/with righteousness') — God's intervention is both powerful and just. The scope expands outward: God is the trust (mivtach) of people at the remotest edges of earth and sea.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, right order, what is correct and fair
key_terms.note: God's awesome deeds are not arbitrary displays of power but expressions of tsedeq — they set things right.

verses.text_kjv: Which by his strength setteth fast the mountains; being girded with power.
verses.rendering:
He sets the mountains in place by his power,
girded with strength.
translator_notes: The verb mekhin ('he establishes, he sets in place') describes the mountains as God's deliberate construction. The image of God being 'girded with strength' (ne'zar bi-gevurah) presents God as a warrior whose belt is raw power — the same power that anchors mountains in bedrock.

verses.text_hebrew: מַשְׁבִּ֤יחַ ׀ שְׁא֣וֹן יַ֭מִּים שְׁא֥וֹן גַּלֵּיהֶ֗ם וַהֲמ֥וֹן לְאֻמִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Which stilleth the noise of the seas, the noise of their waves, and the tumult of the people.
verses.rendering:
He stills the roaring of the seas,
the roaring of their waves,
and the uproar of the nations.
translator_notes: Three things are quieted by the same divine action: the seas (yammim), their waves (galleyhem), and the nations (le'ummim). The parallelism equates cosmic chaos with political turmoil — God subdues both with the same authority. The verb mashbiach ('he stills, he silences') describes decisive calming.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּ֤ירְא֨וּ ׀ יֹשְׁבֵ֣י קְ֭צָוֹת מֵאוֹתֹתֶ֑יךָ מ֤וֹצָאֵֽי־בֹ֖קֶר וָעֶ֣רֶב תַּרְנִֽין׃
verses.text_kjv: They also that dwell in the uttermost parts are afraid at thy tokens: thou makest the outgoings of the morning and evening to rejoice.
verses.rendering:
Those who live at the farthest edges stand in awe at your signs;
you make the gateways of morning and evening shout for joy.
translator_notes: The 'uttermost parts' (qetsavot) are the extreme margins of the inhabited world. God's signs (ototekha) — visible acts of power in nature and history — produce awe even at the world's edges. The 'gateways' (motsa'ey) of morning and evening personify dawn and dusk as shouting with joy (tarnin) — the daily cycle itself celebrates God.

verses.text_hebrew: פָּקַ֥דְתָּ הָאָ֨רֶץ ׀ וַתְּשֹׁ֪קְקֶ֡הָ רַבַּ֬ת תַּעְשְׁרֶ֗נָּה פֶּ֣לֶג אֱ֭לֹהִים מָ֣לֵא מָ֑יִם תָּכִ֥ין דְּ֝גָנָ֗ם כִּי־כֵ֥ן תְּכִינֶֽהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou visitest the earth, and waterest it: thou greatly enrichest it with the river of God, which is full of water: thou preparest them corn, when thou hast so provided for it.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb paqadta ('you visit') is a rich word in Hebrew — when God visits, things change. Here the visit results in saturation (va-teshoqqeha, 'you water it, you overflow it'). The 'stream of God' (peleg Elohim) is not an earthly river but the heavenly source of rain, full of water (male mayim) and ready to pour. God does not merely allow rain; he actively prepares the grain by preparing the earth to receive it. The verb tekhin ('you prepare, you establish') appears twice — once for the grain and once for the earth itself. The farmer works the soil, but God has already worked it from above.
translator_notes: The word peleg ('stream, channel, watercourse') appears with Elohim — 'the channel/stream of God' — suggesting a heavenly irrigation system. The verb tashrennah ('you enrich it') is emphatic: rabbat ('greatly, abundantly'). This is not bare sufficiency but lavish provision.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּלָמֶ֣יהָ רַ֭וֵּה נַחֵ֣ת גְּדוּדֶ֑יהָ בִּרְבִיבִ֥ים תְּ֝מֹגְגֶ֗נָּה צִמְחָ֥הּ תְּבָרֵֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou waterest the ridges thereof abundantly: thou settlest the furrows thereof: thou makest it soft with showers: thou blessest the springing thereof.
translator_notes: The agricultural detail is precise: tlameyha ('its furrows') are soaked, gedudeyha ('its ridges, its clods') are settled and broken down by rain. The verb temoggenah ('you dissolve it, you soften it') describes the rain turning hard, compacted soil into workable earth. Then God blesses the growth (tsimchah, 'its sprouting') — the entire process from plowing to germination is God's work.

verses.text_hebrew: עִ֭טַּרְתָּ שְׁנַ֣ת טוֹבָתֶ֑ךָ וּ֝מַעְגָּלֶ֗יךָ יִרְעֲפ֥וּן דָּֽשֶׁן׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou crownest the year with thy goodness; and thy paths drop fatness.
verses.rendering:
You crown the year with your goodness,
and your wagon tracks drip with abundance.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb ittarta ('you crown') places a wreath on the year itself — the annual agricultural cycle is God's crowned achievement. The image of ma'galekha ('your tracks, your wagon ruts, your paths') dripping with deshen ('fatness, abundance, richness') is vivid: wherever God's chariot has passed, the ground behind it overflows with fertility. God does not merely bless from a distance; he passes through the land, and abundance follows in his tracks.
translator_notes: The word ma'gal ('track, rut, path') can refer to wagon wheel ruts. The image may be of God traveling across the land in a harvest chariot, with the ruts behind him filling with oil and grain. The word deshen ('fatness') is the language of the richest sacrifice and the richest food.

verses.text_hebrew: יִ֭רְעֲפוּ נְא֣וֹת מִדְבָּ֑ר וְ֝גִ֗יל גְּבָע֥וֹת תַּחְגֹּֽרְנָה׃
verses.text_kjv: They drop upon the pastures of the wilderness: and the little hills rejoice on every side.
verses.rendering:
The wilderness pastures drip with moisture,
and the hills wrap themselves in joy.
translator_notes: Even the ne'ot midbar ('pastures of the wilderness') — places normally dry and sparse — participate in the abundance. The hills (geva'ot) 'gird themselves' (tachgornah) with joy (gil) as if putting on a garment. The wilderness and the hills are personified as celebrants dressed for a festival.

verses.text_hebrew: לָבְשׁ֬וּ כָרִ֨ים ׀ הַצֹּ֗אן וַעֲמָקִ֥ים יַעַטְפוּ־בָ֑ר יִ֝תְרוֹעֲע֗וּ אַף־יָשִֽׁירוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The pastures are clothed with flocks; the valleys also are covered over with corn; they shout for joy, they also sing.
translator_notes: The closing verse dresses the landscape in living things: karim ('meadows, pastures') wear flocks like garments, and amaqim ('valleys') are wrapped in grain (bar) like a robe. Then comes the climactic line: yitro'a'u af yashiru ('they shout for joy, indeed they sing!'). The subject is the meadows and valleys themselves — the land has become a choir. The psalm ends not with human praise but with creation's own song.
